title: The Unchanging OR

content.problemStatement:
Chef was recently studying the Bitwise OR operation, and stumbled upon the following problem:

Let F(i)=1 ∣ 2 ∣ 3 ∣ … ∣ iF(i) = 1 \ | \ 2 \ | \ 3 \ | \ \ldots \ | \ iF(i)=1 ∣ 2 ∣ 3 ∣ … ∣ i, where ∣|∣ denotes bitwise OR.

You are given an integer NNN. Find the number of distinct iii such that 2≤i≤N2 \leq i \leq N2≤i≤N and F(i)=F(i−1)F(i) = F(i-1)F(i)=F(i−1).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain an integer TTT — the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains an integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of distinct iii such that 2≤i≤N2 \leq i \leq N2≤i≤N and F(i)=F(i−1)F(i) = F(i-1)F(i)=F(i−1).
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a single line containing an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer – the number of indices i such that 2 ≤ i ≤ N and F(i)=F(i‑1), where F(i)=1|2|…|i.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=2 there is no i (2≤i≤2) with unchanged OR, answer 0. For N=3 only i=3 keeps the OR unchanged, answer 1. For N=4 only i=3 works, answer 1.
N=5: powers of two in [2,5] are {2,4}. Total numbers =4, unchanged count =4‑2=2 (i=3,5). N=10: powers {2,4,8}. Total numbers=9, unchanged=9‑3=6.
Powers of two ≤15 are 2,4,8. Numbers from 2 to 15 are 14, so answer =14‑3=11.

solution.approach: Observe that F(i) changes only when i is a power of two. For any i that is not a power of two, the bits set by previous numbers already cover i, so F(i)=F(i‑1). Therefore, among the numbers from 2 to N, the count of unchanged OR indices equals (N‑1) minus the number of powers of two ≤ N. The number of powers of two ≤ N is floor(log2 N). Hence answer = N - 1 - floor(log2 N). Computing floor(log2 N) can be done in O(1) using the position of the highest set bit.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) total.
